Subject:
NSI Daily Market Report 09/01/2026
Hi Deepesh
We closed up and have opened similar this morning.
In early Asian trade, rising oil prices extended into this morning after spiking on Thursday, as the market bases its prices again on geopolitical risk across several key producers and shipping routes. The risk premium has largely been driven by the continued heightened tensions between the United States and Venezuela, as the US continues to squeeze harder on Venezuela\'s crude movements. On Wednesday, the US had boosted its enforcement against sanctioned Venezuelan oil flows by seizing two Venezuela-linked oil tankers in the Atlantic; one of the seized tankers had been renamed and registered under a Russian flag following a weeks-long pursuit across the Atlantic. Russia had sent a submarine and other naval assets to escort the Russian-flagged VLCC Marinera out of Venezuelan water, in hopes to avoid US detention - though the tanker had been reported to not be carrying any cargo at the time. However, US forces had been pursuing the tanker since December, and were waiting to detain the vessel in Venezuelan waters, reported CBS News earlier this week; the US Justice Department had obtained a seizure warrant due to the ship\'s prior involvement in Iranian oil trade. Considering the fact that the Trump administration had been willing to board a tanker that was apparently under Russian protection, it reflects the extent that the US will go to block any unauthorised shipments, providing a clear warning to the shadow fleet that was used to try avoid sanctions. Additionally, protests in Iran have also contributed towards the risk premium since the rising tensions have led to a nationwide internet blackout and has increased the risk of a major supply shock in the country. After Trump had threatened to rescue any peaceful protesters that are being killed by the Iranian regime, it had boosted concerns in the oil markets that these protests could lead to the US implementing direct action in Iran, resulting in further geopolitical tensions which would only add pressure to the already volatile market. Notably, Turkish Coast Guard assistance and a course diversion had been requested after an oil tanker - that was heading to Russia - had been attacked by a drone in the Black Sea, which no one has claimed responsibility for the attack so far. This event had highlighted that instability in the region is still very prominent, and suggests that there will potentially be widening threats to oil flows. On the other hand, crude oil prices had found broader geopolitical support after Iraq\'s cabinet approved the plans to nationalise operations at the giant West Qurna 2 oilfield, the move based on hopes that Iraq will be able to avoid any potential disruptions that could be caused from the US sanctions on Lukoil. Even though extending operations at one of the world\'s largest oilfields won’t result in an immediate export outage, additional supply uncertainty has sparked at the prospect of potential operational or contractual instability at the oilfield. The conclusion that can be made from all this is that the multiple global tensions have extended geopolitical risk from 2025 into this year, the tumultuous start of the new year has created a lot of uncertainty for traders whilst they navigate through volatile markets. As rising concerns over significant supply disruptions, potentially elevated Venezuelan oil flows (after Trump had claimed that a deal has been agreed upon with the new leadership in the South American country) amid worries of an oversupply, and the uncertainty over how the ongoing geopolitical tensions will play out, have all contributed to the rapidly increasing geopolitical risk, causing prices to be pushed higher, on top of depressing overall market sentiment.
Executives from the shale patch have warned that Trump\'s plans to take over Venezuela’s oil industry could potentially have a negative impact on US oil companies since it may drive oil prices lower, which many shale drillers aren’t able to afford prices dropping much lower, reported Financial Times. Trump has recently suggested that there\'ll be some form of financial guarantees for US oil majors who would be willing to go and operate in Venezuela. Trump had said earlier this week that US companies could be up and running” in Venezuela within 18 months, adding that “A tremendous amount of money will have to be spent, and the oil companies will spend it, and then they’ll get reimbursed by us or through revenue,” but no extensive details have been provided on the guarantees. One unnamed industry executive had told the Financial Times that “We’re talking about this administration screwing us over again,” stating that Trump’s plans for Venezuela were “against American producers... If the US government starts providing guarantees to oil companies to produce or grow oil production in Venezuela I’m going to be . . . pissed”. The chief executive of Latigo Petroleum had also told the Financial Times that “To me, the signal from the administration is: we’d rather spend our American money on propping up a Venezuelan oil business than supporting our current independent businesses”. Moreover, Dan Pickering from Pickering Energy Partners has also commented to the Financial Times that “I think it’s an appropriate reaction by US shale to be miffed... Not just because Venezuelan production might go up but because the US government, in theory, is going to subsidise that.” Trump\'s goals to bring Venezuela’s oil industry back to life can only be achieved through Big Oil majors, since they are the only ones who can afford the required investments on top of being the most likely to withstand lower oil prices, which smaller field operators in the US shale patch cannot afford. Therefore, Trump has set up a meeting with Big Oil executives later today, inviting the heads of commodity trading firms, Vitol and Trafigura, to the White House, in order to discuss the layout and details of his marketing plans for Venezuela\'s oil. These two European firms are some of the largest oil traders in the world, which both have expanded their operations and diversified their portfolios, largely investing in infrastructure such as refineries and storage facilities, on top of being key fuel buyers from major new facilities like the Dangote oil refinery in Nigeria. On average, Vitol has delivered 7.2 million barrels per day of crude oil and other energy products in 2024, whilst Trafigura has roughly handled  4.3 million barrels per day of oil equivalent, representing over 10% of global daily consumption. Notably, as market dynamics evolve, they both have also directed attention towards renewable energy and metals. It is known that Venezuela has the largest proven oil reserves in the world, more than 300 billion barrels. However, due to Western sanctions and several years of mismanagement, underinvestment, and systemic corruption in Venezuela\'s oil sector, the country only produces an average of around 900,000 to 1 million barrels per day, which is significantly lower in comparison to its peak levels of over 3 million barrels per day. Additionally, the Orinoco Belt that\'s located in the eastern Venezuela, holds the majority of the country\'s crude reserves, but the oil in those reserves is extra-heavy and sour with a low API gravity. Consequently, extraction and refining this crude grade is more expensive and challenging compared to lighter, conventional crudes, whilst also requiring to be blended with diluents, like naphtha, in order to be transported and processed in specialized refineries/upgraders. Since US Gulf Coast refineries are configured to process extra-heavy, sour crude, Trump\'s seizure has allowed the United States to control and monopolise Venezuela\'s oil, creating a positive opportunity for the US oil industry, and will likely provide some energy security, particularly as this crude grade is hard to procure elsewhere. However, it is uncertain whether US oil companies will decide to go into Venezuela due to the several risks, one of the main factors being political volatility and instability.
